MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against standard baselines (SVR and bagging) on the same QE datasets (fr-en, en-de) using shared metrics (MAE, RMSE, Pearson) and also introduces probabilistic metrics (NLPD/NLL); hyperparameters are reported and experiments use cross-validation, so the comparisons appear fair. However, the experimental scope is limited (only two language pairs, small QE datasets) and important modern or probabilistic baselines (e.g., Bayesian neural approaches or other calibrated probabilistic regressors) are not included, which prevents the evaluation from being fully comprehensive. The manuscript does discuss limitations of marginal likelihood and dataset size, but would benefit from acknowledging and comparing to stronger probabilistic competitors to substantiate calibration and decision-aware claims. | B(4): The paper compares its probabilistic GP models against standard baselines (SVR and bagging/bootstrapping) on the same QE datasets (fr-en, en-de) and uses appropriate metrics (MAE, RMSE, Pearson r) plus NLPD/NLL to evaluate full predictive distributions; hyperparameters and optimisation (marginal likelihood with 10 restarts) and 10-fold CV are reported, supporting fair comparisons. It also discusses limitations and when prior methods perform comparably, and provides thorough related-work coverage. Minor omissions remain (e.g. no mention of more recent neural QE baselines in the summary), so while comparisons are good and largely fair, they are not fully comprehensive against the very latest alternatives.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against standard baselines (SVR and bagging) on the same QE datasets (fr-en, en-de) using shared metrics (MAE, RMSE, Pearson) and also introduces probabilistic metrics (NLPD/NLL); hyperparameters are reported and experiments use cross-validation, so the comparisons appear fair. However, the experimental scope is limited (only two language pairs, small QE datasets) and important modern or probabilistic baselines (e.g., Bayesian neural approaches or other calibrated probabilistic regressors) are not included, which prevents the evaluation from being fully comprehensive. The manuscript does discuss limitations of marginal likelihood and dataset size, but would benefit from acknowledging and comparing to stronger probabilistic competitors to substantiate calibration and decision-aware claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its probabilistic GP models against standard baselines (SVR and bagging/bootstrapping) on the same QE datasets (fr-en, en-de) and uses appropriate metrics (MAE, RMSE, Pearson r) plus NLPD/NLL to evaluate full predictive distributions; hyperparameters and optimisation (marginal likelihood with 10 restarts) and 10-fold CV are reported, supporting fair comparisons. It also discusses limitations and when prior methods perform comparably, and provides thorough related-work coverage. Minor omissions remain (e.g. no mention of more recent neural QE baselines in the summary), so while comparisons are good and largely fair, they are not fully comprehensive against the very latest alternatives.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a relevant applied problem—quantifying uncertainty in MT quality estimation—and demonstrates that Gaussian Processes with NLPD and asymmetric Bayes estimators can yield operationally useful uncertainty-aware decisions on fr-en and en-de datasets. However, the empirical scope is limited (small QE datasets, only two language pairs) and there is no indication of theoretical guarantees, scalability analysis, or broader calibration evaluation beyond NLPD, which constrains its influence. As a result, the work is a solid, useful contribution that may prompt follow-up research, but it is unlikely to fundamentally change practice or open major new directions without stronger theoretical or large-scale empirical validation. | B(3): This paper tackles an important operational problem—quantifying predictive uncertainty in MT Quality Estimation—by applying principled probabilistic models (GPs, warped GPs) and evaluating with NLPD, which is exactly the kind of evaluation that can change practitioner choices beyond point estimates. The demonstration that uncertainty-aware Bayes estimators for asymmetric losses improve decision-making in optimistic/pessimistic workflows is practically useful and will prompt follow-up work. However, experiments are limited to small QE datasets (fr-en, en-de) and standard feature sets, so while the contribution is solid and enables new directions, its immediate transformative reach across MT and broader NLP is moderate rather than large.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a relevant applied problem—quantifying uncertainty in MT quality estimation—and demonstrates that Gaussian Processes with NLPD and asymmetric Bayes estimators can yield operationally useful uncertainty-aware decisions on fr-en and en-de datasets. However, the empirical scope is limited (small QE datasets, only two language pairs) and there is no indication of theoretical guarantees, scalability analysis, or broader calibration evaluation beyond NLPD, which constrains its influence. As a result, the work is a solid, useful contribution that may prompt follow-up research, but it is unlikely to fundamentally change practice or open major new directions without stronger theoretical or large-scale empirical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important operational problem—quantifying predictive uncertainty in MT Quality Estimation—by applying principled probabilistic models (GPs, warped GPs) and evaluating with NLPD, which is exactly the kind of evaluation that can change practitioner choices beyond point estimates. The demonstration that uncertainty-aware Bayes estimators for asymmetric losses improve decision-making in optimistic/pessimistic workflows is practically useful and will prompt follow-up work. However, experiments are limited to small QE datasets (fr-en, en-de) and standard feature sets, so while the contribution is solid and enables new directions, its immediate transformative reach across MT and broader NLP is moderate rather than large.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from motivation through methodology (GP variants, kernels, marginal likelihood optimisation) to experiments and conclusions, and its main contributions are stated early and consistently revisited. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameter reporting is thorough; figures and tables are described as effective and informative, and the writing quality is good with no major grammatical issues. Minor deductions reflect that some methodological choices (e.g. limited dataset scope to fr-en and en-de) could have been motivated more explicitly for a reader focused on generalisability, and a few sections assume familiarity with GP-specific jargon without brief recaps. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through GP methodology to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (GPs for calibrated QE, use of NLPD, and asymmetric decision rules) are stated early and remain focal throughout. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameter reporting plus cross-validation details suggest careful presentation; figures and tables (e.g., results on fr-en and en-de with NLPD and asymmetric losses) are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage and clearly discussed limitations, though a few minor presentation refinements (e.g., explicit mention of code availability and slightly more exposition around some kernel choices) would make it exceptionally clear.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from motivation through methodology (GP variants, kernels, marginal likelihood optimisation) to experiments and conclusions, and its main contributions are stated early and consistently revisited. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameter reporting is thorough; figures and tables are described as effective and informative, and the writing quality is good with no major grammatical issues. Minor deductions reflect that some methodological choices (e.g. limited dataset scope to fr-en and en-de) could have been motivated more explicitly for a reader focused on generalisability, and a few sections assume familiarity with GP-specific jargon without brief recaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear progression from motivation through GP methodology to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (GPs for calibrated QE, use of NLPD, and asymmetric decision rules) are stated early and remain focal throughout. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameter reporting plus cross-validation details suggest careful presentation; figures and tables (e.g., results on fr-en and en-de with NLPD and asymmetric losses) are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is good with thorough related work coverage and clearly discussed limitations, though a few minor presentation refinements (e.g., explicit mention of code availability and slightly more exposition around some kernel choices) would make it exceptionally clear.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies well-established probabilistic tools (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, ARD/Matèrn kernels) and known evaluation criteria (NLPD/NLL) to the task of MT Quality Estimation and demonstrates practical benefits (including asymmetric Bayes estimators). This is a coherent and useful application but does not introduce new modelling or theoretical contributions (no new GP variants, identifiability/results, or formal guarantees), and primarily repositions existing techniques within QE. The work is therefore an incremental, application-driven contribution that would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts seeking novel methodological advances. | B(3): The paper applies well-established probabilistic methods (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, ARD/Matern kernels) to the QE task and introduces evaluation with NLPD plus asymmetric Bayes estimators for optimistic/pessimistic decision scenarios. This is not a fundamentally new modelling paradigm but is a useful, non-trivial application and perspective shift for QE—especially the emphasis on full predictive distributions and decision-theoretic loss handling on standard fr-en and en-de datasets. The contribution is incremental rather than groundbreaking, but it would inform practitioners and experts by showing concrete benefits of uncertainty-aware evaluation and decision rules over common point-estimate baselines (SVR, bagging).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies well-established probabilistic tools (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, ARD/Matèrn kernels) and known evaluation criteria (NLPD/NLL) to the task of MT Quality Estimation and demonstrates practical benefits (including asymmetric Bayes estimators). This is a coherent and useful application but does not introduce new modelling or theoretical contributions (no new GP variants, identifiability/results, or formal guarantees), and primarily repositions existing techniques within QE. The work is therefore an incremental, application-driven contribution that would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts seeking novel methodological advances.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies well-established probabilistic methods (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, ARD/Matern kernels) to the QE task and introduces evaluation with NLPD plus asymmetric Bayes estimators for optimistic/pessimistic decision scenarios. This is not a fundamentally new modelling paradigm but is a useful, non-trivial application and perspective shift for QE—especially the emphasis on full predictive distributions and decision-theoretic loss handling on standard fr-en and en-de datasets. The contribution is incremental rather than groundbreaking, but it would inform practitioners and experts by showing concrete benefits of uncertainty-aware evaluation and decision rules over common point-estimate baselines (SVR, bagging).

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental work is careful and convincing for demonstrating feasibility (GP variants, kernel/warp exploration, hyperparameter optimisation and CV) but is limited in breadth—only two language pairs, small datasets, no scaling/runtime analysis, limited calibration metrics, and no code release. Given the solid methodology and clear reporting that support the paper’s claims, but acknowledging the gaps that prevent it from being fully exhaustive or deployment-validated, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to demonstrate feasibility (GP variants, kernels, marginal likelihood optimisation with 10 restarts, and evaluation on fr–en and en–de), but they lack breadth and depth demanded by a theory-focused review: only two language pairs, no scaling/runtime analysis, and no explicit calibration metrics beyond NLPD/NLL (e.g. ECE or reliability diagrams) to validate uncertainty claims. Sensitivity/ablation work is limited to kernel/warp choices and random restarts with no systematic hyperparameter or data-size analysis. Related work and limitations are well discussed and hyperparameters are reported, but code is not mentioned, reducing full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a solid experimental treatment: probabilistic GPs (including warped variants and kernel choices) are evaluated with proper hyperparameter optimisation (10 restarts) and 10-fold CV, and the use of NLPD plus concrete asymmetric loss decision rules clearly supports the claims about uncertainty utility. They compare to sensible baselines (SVR) and mention bootstrapping, discuss limitations of marginal likelihood and small QE dataset sizes, and report hyperparameters—helping reproducibility. Minor gaps remain: experiments are limited to two language pairs and small datasets, and while kernel/warp choices are explored there is limited large-scale sensitivity or broader baseline diversity; code release is not mentioned. Overall the empirical work is careful and convincing but not exhaustive, especially for deployment-scale validation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is squarely within ML/NLP scope, uses principled probabilistic methods (GPs/warped GPs) and evaluates uncertainty in a way relevant to the community. I award a 4 because, while methodology and evaluation are solid and appropriate for the venue, the experimental breadth is limited to two small QE datasets and the paper lacks deeper calibration diagnostics and theoretical justification — shortcomings that keep it from a perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper is squarely within the ML/NLP conference scope: probabilistic modelling (Gaussian Processes) for a core NLP task (MT Quality Estimation) and evaluation using predictive distributions (NLPD) addresses community interest in uncertainty-aware models. However, from a theory-purist perspective there are some scope concerns: experiments are limited to two language pairs (fr-en, en-de) on small QE datasets and the paper does not report standard calibration diagnostics (e.g. ECE/reliability diagrams) or provide deeper theoretical justification for the claimed calibration beyond marginal likelihood optimisation. Overall the work targets the right audience and uses expected methods, but the limited experimental breadth and lack of rigorous uncertainty evaluation keep it from a perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it tackles Machine Translation Quality Estimation with principled probabilistic modelling (Gaussian Processes, Warped GPs, ARD/Matern kernels) and exposes predictive uncertainty for downstream decision-making. Methodology and evaluation meet venue standards—marginal likelihood optimisation with 10 restarts, 10-fold cross-validation, and use of NLPD plus asymmetric Bayes estimators for realistic loss scenarios—while comparing to standard baselines (SVR) on common QE datasets (fr-en, en-de). The work is well positioned for the community interested in calibrated predictive distributions and practical utility of uncertainty in NLP pipelines, despite the acknowledged small dataset sizes.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper gives substantial model and evaluation details (GP variants, kernels, optimisation procedure, metrics, 10-fold CV) which aid reproduction, but key elements are missing: the preprocessing pipeline, exact dataset sources/splits, and no code release. These gaps mean reimplementation would require assumptions or contacting the authors, so reproducibility is only partial and meriting a mid-level score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and model configurations (kernels, ARD, Warped GPs, optimisation via marginal likelihood with 10 restarts) and specifies evaluation procedures (NLPD/NLL, MAE, Pearson r, RMSE, 10-fold CV), which aids replication. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described beyond a “standard feature set,” and it is not stated whether the fr–en and en–de QE datasets used are publicly available or exactly which splits were employed. Code is not mentioned, so reimplementation would require inferring implementation details (e.g. exact feature extraction, optimisation settings, seed control), making reproduction only partial.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides many reproducibility-relevant details: GP model variants (Warped GPs, kernels like Matern52/ARD), hyperparameter optimisation via marginal likelihood with 10 random restarts, and evaluation with 10-fold cross-validation and NLPD/asymmetric Bayes estimators. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not clearly described and the exact dataset sources/splits are only named (fr-en, en-de) rather than fully specified, and code is not made available. Given these minor but important gaps, the work is mostly reproducible but would require contacting authors or making assumptions to fully reimplement.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the methodology and empirical work are generally sound and that GPs with warped variants and asymmetric Bayes decision rules are evaluated appropriately, but they also concur that the evaluation is limited by small datasets, lack of standard calibration metrics and statistical tests, and absence of formal theoretical guarantees. Given these substantive limitations on generality and uncertainty evaluation, a conservative mid-level score reflects that the claims are partially supported by the experiments but not fully validated for broader or theoretical certainty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents plausible empirical evidence that GPs (including warped variants) yield better probabilistic QE via NLPD/NLL and demonstrates practical exploitation with asymmetric Bayes estimators; hyperparameters and optimisation details are reported. However, there is no formal theoretical analysis of calibration/consistency or derivations guaranteeing the claimed calibration properties, and the evaluation uses only two language pairs (small datasets) without standard calibration metrics (e.g., ECE, reliability diagrams) or reported statistical tests, limiting confidence in generality. The methodology is largely sound but incomplete from a statistical-theory and uncertainty-evaluation perspective, so the claims are only partially supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's claims are clearly stated and largely supported: probabilistic GPs (including warped variants) are evaluated with appropriate hyperparameter optimisation (10 restarts) and predictive distributions are assessed via NLPD and asymmetric Bayes estimators across 10-fold CV on standard fr-en and en-de QE sets. Mathematical treatment of predictive distributions and asymmetric loss decision rules appears correct and complete in the summary. Methodology is sound in using GPs vs SVR baselines and relevant metrics, but the experiments are limited by small QE datasets and the summary does not mention significance testing or whether baselines were tuned to the same extent, leaving a minor robustness gap. Overall the conclusions about calibrated uncertainty and utility under asymmetric loss are justified, though broader validation (larger datasets / statistical tests / code release) would strengthen the claims.

main_claims: Gaussian Processes (GPs) are suitable probabilistic models for Machine Translation Quality Estimation (QE) that provide well-calibrated uncertainty estimates compared to common point-estimate methods.
Evaluating QE models with Negative Log Predictive Density (NLPD) captures information about the full predictive distribution and can distinguish models with similar point-estimate performance.

Predictive uncertainty can be exploited with asymmetric loss functions (e.g., asymmetric linear and linex losses) to better address practical optimistic and pessimistic decision-making scenarios in translation workflows.
_summary.methodology: Apply Gaussian Process regression (including Warped GPs and various kernels such as Matèrn52 and ARD kernels) to QE using a standard feature set, optimise hyperparameters via marginal likelihood (with 10 random restarts), obtain predictive posterior distributions, and evaluate models using NLPD and asymmetric Bayes estimators derived for AL and linex losses (using 10-fold cross-validation).
limitations: Marginal likelihood (negative log marginal likelihood) does not capture if a model overfits the training data.
QE datasets are typically small (order of thousands), which constrains experimental scale and influences modelling choices.

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a focus on probabilistic modelling and statistical learning theory; extensive experience reviewing for NeurIPS and ICML.
traits: Penalises papers that present primarily empirical comparisons without new theoretical contributions or formal guarantees (deducts points if no derivations/consistency/identifiability results).

Demands rigorous uncertainty evaluation: requires calibration metrics (e.g., ECE, reliability diagrams) and theoretical justification for claimed calibration; lowers score if only one probabilistic metric (NLPD) is used.
Penalises limited experimental scope: deducts points if fewer than 3 diverse datasets/tasks or no analysis of sensitivity to hyperparameter choices beyond anecdotal restarts.
Prefers methods that scale or include complexity/compute analysis; downgrades papers lacking runtime or scalability discussion for real-world deployment.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical and formalist: emphasizes theoretical novelty, rigorous statistical analysis, and broad experimental validation over primarily empirical engineering contributions.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Uncertainty Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in NLP and machine translation with industrial experience deploying MT systems; regular reviewer for ACL and EMNLP.
traits: Rewards clear demonstrations of practical utility: gives credit to papers that show how model outputs (uncertainty) improve real decision-making (e.g., asymmetric loss scenarios).

Values well-executed probabilistic modelling and careful empirical evaluation: rewards use of Bayesian models, proper hyperparameter optimisation (multiple restarts), and cross-validation for robustness.
Rewards adoption of evaluation metrics that capture predictive distributions (e.g., NLPD) and experiments that contrast point-estimates vs full predictive distributions.
Gives positive bias to papers with reproducible experimental setups and standard feature sets/datasets relevant to the application domain.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and impact-oriented: favors papers that convincingly show how probabilistic methods and better evaluation lead to improved decisions in realistic NLP workflows.
